tax: Scree Tumblestone is placed at species rank beneath Tumblestone (Lithic order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a spherical rubble body, a downhill-only attack run, and an inability to climb. It is separated from its sibling the Greater Galeb Duhr by its rolling sphere and downhill rush versus the Galeb Duhr's deliberate two-legged walk; the two share the genus body-plan but never the upright walking. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: loose talus, gravel, and chute-rubble compacted into a rolling ball.
ap: Scree Tumblestone stands 1–1.5 m across and a few hundred kilograms. Its silhouette is a rough sphere of packed rubble that flattens slightly at rest. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a banded surface where finer gravel has migrated outward under spin; a hollow grinding roar audible before it is seen, rising in pitch as it accelerates; and no fixed face, but a brief dark gap that opens uphill to draw in fresh rubble. It moves with a downhill roll that builds to running pace, helpless on the flat and unable to climb, and shows none; it is recognised by sound and the dust-plume it throws up. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a swept, gravel-scoured groove down the centre of a chute and a rubble fan at the bottom, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Scree Tumblestone persists in steep talus chutes and rockfall gullies on the Tier II–III margin, where gravity does its hunting for it. It does not breed; it forms where earth-pressure pulses through a loaded talus chute and binds the loose stone mid-slip, and recurs whenever a fresh rockfall reloads the chute and the next pressure-pulse rebuilds it. It sustains its mass on gravitational potential of loaded slopes, drawing in loose rubble swept up as it rolls, replacing what it sheds. Range is bounded by the bottom of its chute: outside that band the body disintegrates into an ordinary rubble fan on reaching the flat. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a chute that rattles and self-sorts its gravel without any creature visible, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Scree Tumblestone is roused by vibration upslope of it — a footstep, a dropped pack, a tremor and otherwise lies settled as an innocuous gravel mound at a chute head. It claims the length of a single chute and answers intrusion with releasing downhill to flatten whatever is below. Toward grey-folk it is purely reactive; survey crews report that Lanternkin learn never to rest at the foot of a sorted-gravel chute. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Scree Tumblestone: a body of loose talus, gravel, and chute-rubble compacted into a rolling ball; immune to bludgeoning and breaks apart only to reform; and a defining weakness — any barrier that stops its roll, after which it collapses inert. Its senses run through the medium itself: vibration only, read uphill through the loaded slope. It tolerates dust, heat, and darkness but fails at flat ground and standing water, both of which arrest it. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the spherical body and downhill-only run, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Scree Tumblestone are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it accelerates to running pace down any loaded chute; it absorbs loose rubble to repair mid-roll; it throws a blinding dust-plume ahead of itself; and it can pause as a gravel mound to ambush. Each is a direct expression of gravity acting on a self-binding rubble sphere, so it cannot, for instance, climb, cross level ground, or strike anything above it. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Scree Tumblestone matters because it turns every steep chute into a one-way trap, and crews must cross talus margins above the load line, never below. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as a slope hazard logged with the chute, not the open passage, and its presence reshapes routes through the Tier II–III talus chutes, now crossed high and never rested beneath. Wall-Wardens map active chutes by the swept central groove a Tumblestone leaves.
g: Scree Tumblestone (Saxum rotundans) is a fast lithic elemental that bundles loose talus into a self-rolling sphere and runs downhill. Documented from the steep talus chutes between Tier II and Tier III, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a packed sphere of fist-sized rubble bound by earth-pressure — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the only lithic elemental that attacks by momentum rather than mass, reaching a sprinter's pace down a chute. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a sudden fan of loose rubble strewn across the chute mouth and no carcass.
